texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We introduce the notion of empirical coordination for quantum correlations. Quantum mechanics enables the calculation of probabilities for experimental outcomes, emphasizing statistical averages rather than detailed descriptions of individual events. Empirical coordination is thus a natural framework for quantum systems. Focusing on the cascade network, the optimal coordination rates are established, indicating the minimal resources required to simulate on average a quantum state. As we consider a network with classical communication links, superposition cannot be maintained, hence the quantum correlations are separable (i.e., a convex combination of product states). This precludes entanglement. Providing the users with shared randomness, before communication begins, does not affect the optimal rates for empirical coordination. We begin with a rate characterization for a basic two-node network, and then generalize to a cascade network. The special case of a network with an isolated node is considered as well. The results can be further generalized to other networks as our analysis includes a generic achievability scheme. The optimal rate formula involves optimization over a collection of state extensions. This is a unique feature of the quantum setting, as the classical parallel does not include optimization. As demonstrated through examples, the performance depends heavily on the choice of decomposition. We further discuss the consequences of our results for quantum cooperative games.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent advances in the Internet of Things (IoT) have drawn attention to the role of coordination in networks with diverse topologies [1, 2]. Cuff et al. [3] presented a general information-theoretic model for network coordination, where as opposed to traditional coding tasks, the objective is not to exchange messages between network nodes but rather generate correlation [4]. Two types of coordination tasks were introduced in the classical framework [3]. In strong coordination, the users produce actions in order to simulate a product distribution. That is, the joint distribution resembles that of a particular memoryless source [5]. Empirical coordination imposes a weaker and less stringent condition compared to strong coordination. It requires the type, i.e., the frequency of actions, to converge into a desired distribution [6]. There are many information-theoretic tasks that are closely related to coordination, such as channel/source simulation [7-12], randomness extraction [13, 14], entanglement distirbution [15], state transformation [16, 17], state merging [18, 19], entanglement dilution [20-23], and compression [24-29].

There are many information-theoretic tasks that are closely related to coordination, such as channel/source simulation [7-12], randomness extraction [13, 14], entanglement distirbution [15], state transformation [16, 17], state merging [18, 19], entanglement dilution [20-23], and compression [24-29].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Empirical coordination and its variations are widely studied in the classical information theory literature. Le Treust [4] considered joint source-channel empirical coordination. Le Treust and Bloch [30] further used empirical coordination as a unified perspective for masking, amplification, and parameter estimation at the receiver. Cuff and Zhao [31] studied empirical coordination using implicit communication, with information embedding applications, such as digital watermarking, steganography, cooperative communication, and strategic play in team games. Cervia et al. [32] devised a polar coding scheme for empirical coordination. Related models can also be found in [33, 34].

texts.orig: Quantum mechanics enables the calculation of probabilities for experimental outcomes, emphasizing statistical averages rather than detailed descriptions of individual events. For instance, the Heisenberg uncertainty principle states that the standard deviations of position and momentum cannot be minimized simultaneously [35]. Some scholars, such as Fuchs and Peres [36], contend that the quantum theory describes physical reality at all, but is instead confined to representing statistical correlations [37]. Empirical coordination is thus a natural framework for quantum systems.
texts.text: Quantum mechanics enables the calculation of probabilities for experimental outcomes, emphasizing statistical averages rather than detailed descriptions of individual events. For instance, the Heisenberg uncertainty principle states that the standard deviations of position and momentum cannot be minimized simultaneously [35]. Some scholars, such as Fuchs and Peres [36], contend that the quantum theory describes physical reality at all, but is instead confined to representing statistical correlations [37]. Empirical coordination is thus a natural framework for quantum systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Empirical coordination also plays a role in quantum data compression [38]. The optimal compression rate for a general ensemble of mixed states is unknown [39]. Barnum et al. [40] have reformulated the model for a source of commuting density operators, and Kramer and Savari [25] developed a rate-distortion theory that unifies the visible and blind approaches (cf. [41] and [42]). Coordination of separable correlations with classical links is described as follows. Consider a network of K nodes, where Node i performs an encoding operation E i on a system A i , for i ∈ [ K ] . Some of the nodes are connected by one-way classical links. We denote the rate limit for the link from Node i to Node j by R i,j . Before the coordination protocol begins, the nodes may also share common randomness (CR). Furthermore, some of the nodes can have access to side information. The objective in the coordination problem is to establish a specific correlation, i.e., to simulate a desired quantum state ω A 1 ...A K . Since the links are classical, the correlation is separable. The optimal performance is defined by the communication rates that are necessary and sufficient for simulating the desired correlation on average.

texts.orig: We define empirical coordination for quantum states, and discuss the justification for our definition and its physical interpretation. We focus on the cascade network and determine the optimal coordination rates, which represent the minimal resources for the empirical simulation of a separable state among multiple parties. The cascade setting is depicted in Figure 1. Alice, Bob, and Charlie wish to simulate a separable state ω ABC . They are provided with rate-limited communication links, R 1 2 from Alice to Bob, and R 2 3 from Bob to Charlie.

texts.orig: In Section II, we set our notation conventions. In Section III, we present the definitions for our model and their physical interpretation. Section IV is dedicated to the results, including the statement about CR and the capacity theorems for the twonode network, the cascade network and the isolated node. Section V and Section VI provide the achievability and converse analysis, respectively. Section VII concludes with a discussion about the comparison between strong coordination and empirical coordination, as well as the implications of our results on quantum cooperative games.

texts.orig: Let System A be associated with H A . The quantum state of A is described by a density operator ρ A ∈ L ( H A ) , i.e., a unit-trace positive semidefinite operator. Let ∆( H A ) denote the set of all such operators. The probability distribution of a measurement outcome is derived from a positive operator-valued measure (POVM). In finite dimensions, this reduces to a finite set of positive semidefinite operators { D j : j ∈ [ N ] } that satisfy ∑ N j =1 D j = ✶ , where ✶ denotes the identity operator. By the Born rule, the probability of a measurement outcome j is given by p J ( j ) = Tr( D j ρ A ) , for j ∈ [ N ] .

texts.orig: Suppose that Alice receives a random sequence X n , drawn from a memoryless (i.i.d.) source ∼ p X . This can be viewed as side information Alice obtains from a local measurement on her environment. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this subsection, we discuss the justification and the physical interpretation of our coordination criterion. As mentioned in the Introduction, quantum mechanics enables the calculation of probabilities for experimental outcomes, emphasizing statistical averages rather than detailed descriptions of individual events. For instance, the Heisenberg uncertainty principle states that the standard deviations of position and momentum cannot be minimized simultaneously [35]. Some scholars, such as Fuchs and Peres [36], contend that quantum theory describe physical reality at all, but is instead confined to representing statistical correlations [37]. Empirical coordination is thus a natural framework for quantum systems. Further justification is provided below.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A. Summary
texts.text: A. Summary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have introduced the notion of empirical coordination for quantum correlations. Quantum mechanics enables the calculation of probabilities for experimental outcomes, emphasizing statistical averages rather than detailed descriptions of individual events. Empirical coordination is thus a natural framework for quantum systems. Focusing on the cascade network, we established the optimal coordination rates, indicating the minimal resources for the empirical simulation of a quantum state. As we consider a network with classical communication links, superposition cannot be maintained, hence the quantum correlations are separable. This precludes entanglement. We have shown that providing the users with shared randomness, before communication begins, does not affect the optimal rates for empirical coordination (see Theorem 1). We began with the rate characterization for the basic two-node network (Theorem 2), and then generalized to a cascade network (Theorem 3). The special case of a network with an isolated node was addressed as well (see Corollary 4). The results generalize to other networks as our analysis includes a generic achievability scheme (see Lemma 5) . Nonetheless, we do not claim to have solved all coordination scenarios or network topologies.
texts.text: We have introduced the notion of empirical coordination for quantum correlations. Quantum mechanics enables the calculation of probabilities for experimental outcomes, emphasizing statistical averages rather than detailed descriptions of individual events. Empirical coordination is thus a natural framework for quantum systems. Focusing on the cascade network, we established the optimal coordination rates, indicating the minimal resources for the empirical simulation of a quantum state. As we consider a network with classical communication links, superposition cannot be maintained, hence the quantum correlations are separable. This precludes entanglement. We have shown that providing the users with shared randomness, before communication begins, does not affect the optimal rates for empirical coordination (see Theorem 1). We began with the rate characterization for the basic two-node network (Theorem 2), and then generalized to a cascade network (Theorem 3). The special case of a network with an isolated node was addressed as well (see Corollary 4). The results generalize to other networks as our analysis includes a generic achievability scheme (see Lemma 5) . Nonetheless, we do not claim to have solved all coordination scenarios or network topologies.

texts.orig: B. Game-theoretic implications
texts.text: B. Game-theoretic implications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In many cooperative games, the payoff is associated with the correlation between the players. In the penny matching game , as introduced by Gossner et al. [48], Alice receives a classical sequence x n from an i.i.d source, thereafter Alice and Bob produce sequences a n and b n that should be close to one another and to x n as well. In other words, Alice and Bob try to guess the source sequence one bit at a time. They gain a point for every bit they both guess correctly. Alice's action a n is referred to as a guess, even though she knows the original source sequence x n . As it turns out, an optimal strategy could let Alice guess wrong, i.e., a i = x i , for some of the time [48]. Cuff and Zhao [31] analyzed a generalized version of the game through the classical two-node network. Here, we introduce a quantum version of the game.

Alice and Bob can then reach an average payoff γ ≥ 0 asymptotically, if and only if Alice can send a message to Bob at rate R 1 2 > C 2-node ( ω ) for some ω AB ∈ SEP( γ ) . The optimal rate C 2-node ( ω ) is characterized by Theorem 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have shown that CR does not improve the coordination capacity. That is, if R ℓ j is achievable with CR, it is also achievable without CR. We provide an intuitive explanation below. Suppose we use a coding scheme where the CR element is a sequence U n , drawn from a memoryless source p U over U , and each user encodes by a collection of maps {E ( u ) } , taking u = U i at time i . Then, this CR-assisted coding scheme can be replaced with a code based on a fixed agreed upon sequence ˜ u n of type ˆ P ˜ u n ≈ p U .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: E. Applications
texts.text: E. Applications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent advances in machine-to-machine communication [6] and the Internet of Things (IoT) [1] have raised interest in networks with various topologies [2]. These network topologies are relevant for various applications, such as distributed computing [50], autonomous vehicles [51], embedded sensors [52], players in a cooperative game [31], and quantum-enhanced IoT [53, 54]. Coordination with classical links is motivated by quantum-enhanced IoT networks in which the communication links are classical [53-56]. The problem at hand is to find the optimal transmission rates required in order to establish a desired correlation. Empirical coordination also plays a role in quantum data compression [38, 40, 42]. The optimal compression rate for a quantum source of pure states was first established by Schumacher [57] for a quantum source of pure states (see also [58, 59]). However, the problem remains unsolved for a general ensemble of mixed states [39]. Barnum et al. [40] have reformulated the model for a source of commuting density operators. The problem can be divided into two scenarios: in the visible scenario, the encoder has access to the entire sequence of quantum states [41], whereas in the blind scenario, the encoder only has access to a sequence of measurements but not the actual states [42]. Kramer and Savari [25] developed a rate-distortion theory that unifies those variants. Rate distortion can be viewed as a special case of empirical coordination.

The optimal compression rate for a quantum source of pure states was first established by Schumacher [57] for a quantum source of pure states (see also [58, 59]). However, the problem remains unsolved for a general ensemble of mixed states [39]. Barnum et al. [40] have reformulated the model for a source of commuting density operators. The problem can be divided into two scenarios: in the visible scenario, the encoder has access to the entire sequence of quantum states [41], whereas in the blind scenario, the encoder only has access to a sequence of measurements but not the actual states [42]. Kramer and Savari [25] developed a rate-distortion theory that unifies those variants. Rate distortion can be viewed as a special case of empirical coordination.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: F. Future directions
texts.text: F. Future directions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In another work by the authors [43], we have also considered strong coordination in a network with quantum links. This allows for the generation of multipartite entanglement and is closely related to tasks such as quantum channel/source simulation [7-12, 60], state merging [18, 19], state redistribution [61, 62], zero-communication state transformation [16, 17], entanglement dilution [20-23], randomness extraction [13, 14], source coding [24-29], and many others. An interesting avenue for future research is to study empirical coordination in such networks. There are many other coordination scenarios and network topologies that could be studied further, e.g., empirical coordination with entanglement assistance. Other interesting directions include the one-shot setting ( n = 1 ) and coordination with two-way communication.
